10:08 PM
The campaign has concluded. Some have fallen, but more have found great glory, and the King's person is assuredly safe once more. The upstart English nobles slip back to the coast, having learned their lesson about French force-of-arms.
Narrator 12/13/2021 1:20 PM
The Spring Campaign
1:21 PM
As the siege of La Rochelle proceeds, the English show no signs of abandoning their attempts to relieve the city. Week after week, ships of all sizes attempt to slip through the blockade, and enough succeed that the city continues to maintain its resistance, despite all odds.
1:26 PM
Spies in Cornwall have reported an unusual gathering of fishing boats backed up by the English Admiralty. The newly created office of the First Lord of the Admiralty, held by the newly-elevated Baron of Nayland, has put together plans for a multi-prong distraction and resupply mission. While the French military would normally be hard-pressed to resist such a concentration, even without English army support, the volunteers out of Paris have been unusually enthusiastic this season, and the Cardinal orders the hodgepodge of regiments deployed in force.
Narrator 12/13/2021 1:40 PM
The French forces include: Bg. General @Ghôst Touchard commanding the Royal Northern Highland Border regiment. Marquis Col. @Curtis Sinnoch, commanding the Royal Foot Guards, with Baron Lt. Col. @Jules Lavelle commanding the 1st Battalion, and Pvt. @Joseph Bousain serving in C Company. Pvt. @Slade Imbee, serving in the King's Musketeers F Company under Lt. Col. Adhemar Lucy (MA 5). Col. Raoul LaRue (MA 2), commanding the Frontier Regiment, with Lt. Col. @Jean Renault and his Crown Prince Cuirassiers 1st Squadron, and Cpts. @Blastagarded and @Abelard Soucy leading companies. (edited)
Narrator
used /roll
Dice Golem BOT 12/13/2021 1:41 PM
1d6: (6) = 6
Narrator 12/13/2021 1:42 PM
By the time the French forces have made the march from Paris, the English have already made their landing. They've landed on beaches up and down the coast, and are transporting convoys of goods overland to reach the city, the sea routes being too heavily defended. It falls to the regimental commanders to find a plan to clear the beaches, brush, and coastal forests of the enemy.
1:43 PM
The enemy forces are primarily English sea dogs, hearty men who've cut their teeth battling pirates in the Caribbean, many of whom saw their first service under Sir Francis Drake himself.
1:44 PM
As the ranking officer, @Ghôst Touchard should lay out the plan of deployment with @Curtis Sinnoch advising.
1:45 PM
Col. LaRue agrees to follow Bg. Gen. Thibodeaux's advice in all matters.
1:50 PM
Correction: @Ghôst Touchard commands a battalion under Col. Matthieu Barrault (MA 4).
1:50 PM
@Curtis Sinnoch, the deployment plan is up to you.
Curtis Sinnoch 12/13/2021 1:51 PM
Overall deployment of all forces, or just the RFG?
Narrator 12/13/2021 1:51 PM
Overall -- the other colonels are not bound by it, but will respect your seniority.
1
Curtis Sinnoch 12/13/2021 2:10 PM
"My glorious compatriots and men-at-arms! Once again, the villainous English are attempting to subvert France herself and corrupt our people! We will show them no quarter, nor should we expect any! The Royal Foot Guard will set up in the forests along with the King's Musketeers - we will ambush and pick off any unexpecting forces. Baron Lt. Col, @Jules Lavelle , be on eye in particular for any caravans transporting weapons and ammunition - you have full permission to engage with extreme force as you see fit." "Col. LaRue - if your men can harass the English as they unload on the beaches - set upon them before they can defend themselves, and then withdraw to the dunes and forests when they attempt to counter, we will have Lt. Col. @Jean Renault's Calvary ready to tear them apart with a counter charge." "Col. Matthieu Barrault, your fine soldiers are some of the best France has to offer. With the might and brains of @Ghôst Touchard, I believe you have the ability to break any force the Sea Dogs launch at us. Whereas the Frontier Regiment will operate as a hammer throwing their ranks into discord, you shall operate as a scalpel, sabotaging landing spots, eliminating officers, and engaging in any targets of opportunity you may find - my only request is to arrange with captains @Blastagarded and @Abelard Soucy any beach operations you perform to make sure we have an adequate rearguard in the event they attempt any trickery of their own. TO THE GLORY OF FRANCE!"
4
1
Narrator 12/13/2021 2:15 PM
@Curtis Sinnoch, roll for your battle result
Curtis Sinnoch
used /roll
Dice Golem BOT 12/13/2021 2:16 PM
1d6: (4) = 4
Narrator 12/13/2021 2:29 PM
The French forces follow their orders nearly to the letter, repulsing the enemy wherever they encounter him. There are losses, yes, but these are the glorious deaths of battle, not the shameful ones of cowardice. Curtis sits atop his horse, repressing a smirk, as every successive explosion and cheer heralds French triumph, and the English dogs returning to the sea that whelped them. Your result is 2 - Enemy driven from field.
2:29 PM

Narrator 12/13/2021 3:42 PM
The English lack any of the polish or refinement of the French, battling like madmen, stinking of rum and their own excrement as they come out of the woods in endless lines, gap-toothed grins and wide eyes the last sight many of the gallant French soldiers see. Not Bg. Gen. Thibodeaux, though, who leads a fighting retreat back to the beach, shooting and stabbing his way from horseback, carving a bloody path even through the line of Reavers who stand basking in their slaughter of the Frontier Regiment. Finally given room to breathe, Thibodeaux sets his back to the sea. His horse covered in lather, he sees the hordes of hideous English preparing to deliver him to his maker. The Brigadier-General and his companions realize this is their moment, and set to charge. To their shock, as one, the reavers turn and flee inland. Thibodeaux and the other survivors realize that French valor has cowed their feral foe once again. They never see the galleon rise up out of the surf behind them, carried high on a freak wave, slamming down into the gathered Frenchmen below. Onlookers report a loud slapping sound of wood on sand, and then eerie silence. (edited)

Curtis Sinnoch 12/13/2021 3:47 PM
Curtis, watching through a spyglass several kilometers away, watches as the galleon smash into the sand and erupt into the base planks and boards that formerly composed a ship. He sighs, shakes his head ruefully, then straightens
3:49 PM
"Send a message to his Grace the Cardinal. While we have won this day, there has been... a vacancy that needs to be addressed. We need to ensure it goes to a man of proper upbringing and valor to honor the memory of good Thibodeaux"

The Cardinal concludes that a Marquis outranks a Knight, and passes his recommendation to the Minister of War, who orders the promotion to Brevet Brigadier-General for Marquis Sinnoch.

While we wait, @Blastagarded, your rolls are Death 6, Mention 7, Promotion 6. No chance for loot.
Blastagarded 12/13/2021 4:24 PM
Jean knew going to war was a risk, but this wasn’t the first conflict he’d been tasked with observing. And the potential benefit to securing a better and more comfortable existence in this barbaric hellhole he was stranded in seemed worth it. However, as Jean found out, it is one thing to be a temporal observer of a war and another thing entirely to be a participant. As soon as he stepped onto the field of battle, the temporal database went berserk. With the butterfly effect wreaking its havoc and Jean witnessing Frenchman everywhere laying down their lives for a hard won fight, the probability map for the outcome as a costless victory for the French dropped from Reasonable Certainty to Flux. All bets were now off. Both for his survival and that of the version of the future he came from. Records from the 1600s were sparse for details as to how events actually unfolded. And it could merely be that the historical records were wrong. The timeline may be fine and the bloodless accounts from this era were simply the result of a bias by those who wrote the ending. But with the battle engaged, there was no time to determine if and how his presence could alter things so significantly. Survive first. Fix later. “Alright men, follow my lead,” Jean shouted, “Our charge will take us perilously close to the enemy before we dismount. In fact, I encourage you all to plow your horse into the enemy as you leap from the saddle.” With that, Jean led the charge, hoping an overwhelming show of devastation would demoralize the enemy into leaving him be. As he closed in on the carnage, Jean used his enhanced strength and agility to leap from the saddle. While his horse trampled over two men, his landing coincided with his blade cutting down another Englishman who was too dumbstruck by the scene to react. The battle joined, all that was left was for the time traveler to hope fate would favor him.

Narrator 12/13/2021 4:36 PM
The fighting is awful, far worse than any entertainment or historical reenactment could have prepared him for. Jean kills, and kills again, and soon his vision is nothing but metal and blood, and his mind focuses on nothing more than the endless, repetitive cycle of dodging, cutting, and trying to ignore the awful stench. His awareness returns as the rank-and-file drop away and he finds himself squaring off with an enemy captain, a man of uncommon cleanliness compared to the rest of the ragged foe. He bears medals and other decorations on his sailing coat, and strikes with a finesse and lightness uncharacteristic of the savage cutlass blows of his fellows. Jean and the Englishman strike back and forth at each other, making little progress, until Jean sees an opening. Allowing his opponent to overcommit, he steps on the man's foot and shoves with his shoulder, stopping his opponent from catching himself and knocking him to the ground. He kicks the young captain's sword loose, then raises his own blade for the coup de grace. Reports from nearby soldiers are scattered and confused as to what came next. All agree, though, that there was a bright light, as if from nowhere, and strange words warning of 'temporal paradox'. A moment later, Jean's body seemed to vaporize, exploding into particles of quickly-dissipating light that swirled up into the sky, leaving nothing behind but an astonished English captain.

Joseph Bousain 12/13/2021 4:59 PM
As the Royal Foot Guards march towards the battle, Pierre feels that everything looks all too familliar. He ruminates on this strange feeling for a long time before it dawns on him that this was the exact situation which faced General Antonius during the Sicilian Revolt of 268. After a moment or two of contemplation, he recalls the various crucial mistakes which Antonius made on that occassion. "Oh dear, I must warn Curtis" he mutters to himself. After glancing one way and then the other to check that there aren't any sergants nearby to catch him, he dashes off the road and into the bushes, in the vague direction which he imagined the Colonel to be. Unfortunately for Pierre, he bumps directly into Sergant Fellipe. "Right sonny, trying to desert are we? I know just what to do with you." Just as Pierre begins the explain the similarity between the present situation and that of 268, and the consequent necessity of protecting the army's left flank, the sergant wallops Pierre about the face and drags him to the front of the line. "Right sonny, now you can go where the real fighting is"

Anton looks around. The battlefield is...an utter trash heap of misery and all the worst parts of humanity. It feels like he belongs, for some inexplicable reason. With a faint smile, his heart swells and he sidles into battle.

Narrator 12/13/2021 6:43 PM
Anton somehow escapes the slaughter of his new allies in the penal battalion, fighting through to an opening in the lines of reavers, and from there making his way up the beach. Determined to find some other French unit to attach himself to, he is drawn to the sound of whispered French voices. A thousand paces up the beach, he finds a small lagoon set back from a little shoal, almost totally hidden by tree cover. Only the sound of voices leads him to find the cove with its little skiff loaded with crates and barrels. The peasants working the docks seem shocked to see him. Quickly examining the goods, Anton sees they're a mix of English and French origin, all military materiél. "Looters..." he utters, as the burly peasants serving as the muscle approach, wood cudgels in hand. Anton falls back, bringing up his musket wildly, and gets off a shot, taking off the crown of the lead peasant. The rest scatter at the sound, leaving Anton to investigate the little smuggler's dock on his own. Creeping forward, he steps onto the little skiff, finding his sea legs as it rocks back and forth. One crate seems larger than the others, its corners marked with red paint. Curious, he grabs the edge, pulling softly. There is a click and a hiss, and the box unfolds to reveal a smuggler's cannon, rigged to go off and pointing straight at him. He tries to replace the cover, and there is a great explosion. The cannonball travels through his chest, soaring out over the beach, streaked red and white with his blood and bones. Three hundred meters away, the Sergant shoves Pierre forward towards the front line. A random cannonball sails into the space just then occupied by his head, and removes it. The battle continues much as it had before. (edited)

Narrator 12/15/2021 12:41 PM
A single field army will be deployed, with every brigade except the Horse Guards and the 1st Brigade of Foot.
12:42 PM
This will be a Siege campaign.
Narrator 2/5/2022 1:27 PM
The Summer Campaign
1:31 PM
The siege of La Rochelle continues. The English navy, under the Admiral of the Fleet, the Earl of Lindsey, has assembled a flotilla of 29 warships and 31 merchantmen, ready to force their way past the sea wall and either break the siege, or evacuate the city entirely.
1:34 PM
It is the largest naval assault ever conducted against the French homeland. If the English are able to breach the seawall, a fortification devised by Cardinal Richelieu himself, they will have direct access to the city, and the rebellion will spread like a cancer. This is the last chance to contain the Protestant venom.
1:35 PM
The Cardinal himself has taken to the field, and will command the army on this campaign. He has positioned his headquarters on the seawall itself, and has consecrated every stone as a fortress in defense of God and France.
1:35 PM
Narrator 2/5/2022 1:47 PM
The Field Army consists of the following: 1st Division: Lt. Gen. Bernard Lefebvre
  • Guards Brigade: Bg. Gen. Alphonse Joseph Leclerc
  • - Royal Foot Guards: Lt. Col. @Jules Lavelle
  • - Cardinal's Guard
  • - King's Musketeers: Lt. Col. Adhemar Lucy
  • - - F Company enlisted: Sub. @Slade Imbee
  • RNH 2nd Division: Lt. Gen. Frank Bouchard
  • 2nd Brigade of Foot: Bg. Gen. Xavier Cherbuliez
  • - 13th Fusiliers
  • - 53rd Fusiliers
  • 3rd Brigade of Foot
  • - 27th Musketeers
  • - 4th Arquebusiers
  • 4th Brigade of Foot
  • - 69th Arqubusiers
  • - Gascon Regiment Frontier Division Cavalry Division: Lt. Gen. Denis Nee
  • Heavy Brigade: Bg. Gen. Jean-Martin Desjardins
  • - Crown Prince Cuirassiers: Col. Gaël Roquebert
  • - - 1st Squadron: Lt. Col. @Jean Renault
  • - Archduke Leopold Cuirassiers
  • Dragoon Brigade
1:49 PM
The Cardinal addresses his officers. "So long as the Huguenots have a foothold in France, the King will never be master within his own borders or be able to enter upon any glorious undertaking without. It is incumbent upon us this day, with the English fleet bearing down on our defenses with the full fury of the Adversary on their side, that we put down this menace to Christian, and preserve the security of France, her people, and her God forevermore. Amen!"
1:50 PM
The Cardinal delivers his orders, arranging the army in a horseshoe pattern to catch any stray English in their net.
1:50 PM
Narrator
used /roll
Dice Golem BOT 2/5/2022 1:50 PM
1d6: (1) = 1
Narrator 2/5/2022 1:52 PM
For all his fervor, the Cardinal has little military background. The English admiral, noting the broad array facing his forces, opts to concentrate his push on one section of the seawall, wreaking tremendous damage. The result is 5 - Broken by sortie.
1:54 PM
Lt. Gen. Lefebvre believes he understands the English mind well. He instructs his elite units to put their sharpshooting to the test, to try to pick off English officers aboard the attacking ships.
Narrator
used /roll
Dice Golem BOT 2/5/2022 1:54 PM
1d6: (2) = 2
Narrator 2/5/2022 1:56 PM
His plan fails; skilled as the men are, the rolling waves, and huddled-together English soldiery, makes it nearly impossible to distinguish common man from officer. Still, the bullets do find home in English flesh. The result is 4 - Inconclusive.
1:58 PM
Bg. Gen. Leclerc, realizing the full might of the English command staff will remain to direct the attack, orders his section of the seawall abandoned. His adjutant stares like Leclerc has gone mad, but he hushes the young officer and continues with the orderly evacuation.
Narrator
used /roll
Dice Golem BOT 2/5/2022 1:58 PM
1d6: (4) = 4
Narrator 2/5/2022 2:00 PM
As the English ships collide with the empty section of the seawall, disgorging their howling passengers, the planned charge across the fortification is halted by the realization that the mortar for this part of the seawall has yet to fully settle! The English charge back onto their boats before the weight of the landing causes them to settle entirely. The attack is partly slowed, but not stopped. The result is 3 - Inconclusive.
2:01 PM

The shots are no more damaging than before, though no great harm befalls his men. The result is 3 - Inconclusive.
2:10 PM
As the 2nd Division maintains the rest of the seawall, the Cavalry Division finds itself milling about on land. Lt. Gen. Denis Nee has been ordered to 'use his discretion' in choosing where and how to meet the enemy. With little idea how the English might attack, he has chosen to arrange the Heavy Brigade on the northern shore, and the Dragoons Brigade on the southern. If the English attempt a landing, his men will be ready.
Narrator
used /roll
Dice Golem BOT 2/5/2022 2:10 PM
1d6: (2) = 2
Narrator 2/5/2022 2:12 PM
Unfortunately, while his men are ready, Nee is not -- he is still arranging the Dragoons to the south when half of the English fleet redirects its efforts from the failing seawall to make a landing on the northern shore. The result is 5 - Broken by sortie. (edited)
2:15 PM
As the English disembark, Bg. Gen. Desjardins frantically considers how he can possibly repel an infantry charge with a couple regiments of horse. He dimly recalls an old military text that recommended something called gorilla tactics. He doesn't know quite what it means, but orders his men to ride into the woods, which he imagines is where a gorilla would prefer to fight.

Over half the English follow the Heavy Brigade away from the shore, their strategic concerns overtaken by bloodlust. Unable to catch up with the horse, they accomplish little. It is not a glorious victory, but it is a real one for the Heavy Brigade. The result is 3 - Inconclusive.

3:51 PM
The rest of the battle proceeds similarly -- having gotten the lay of the land, the English then pull back without taking serious losses. The siege continues, but the core of the English fleet remains a threat, and retreats to the open sea to regroup. The Cardinal declares the day an enormous success. As the seawall is hastily reinforced, cries of despair can be heard from within the city proper. The Huguenots know their days are numbered.

Jules Lavelle 2/5/2022 9:20 PM
After the battle, as you are nursing a musket wound, the Cardinal notices you. He shakes your hand, his eyes steely, and continues down the line of injured mine. As you are gathering up your awe, his attendant drops a purse of 400 crowns into your hand. "Remember his Eminence's generosity, and our great victory," he hisses, and follows after his master.
Slade Imbee 2/5/2022 9:28 PM
Slade is honored and dumbfounded. "feared the worst." He thinks. "Our near shameful marksmanship failed to deprive the English of their commanders as planned. Though we were victorious, it was not the rout that was planned for this day. I thought we would be punished. The Lord surely smiles on us and graces the Cardinal and his Eminence with generosity indeed!".* Smiling ear-to-ear he turns to move on to his next duty with exuberance when a twinge in his thigh halts him. "Oh right... perhaps a medic for first to bandage this up"

Narrator 3/28/2022 11:01 PM
The Fall Campaign
Narrator
used /roll
Dice Golem BOT 3/28/2022 11:01 PM
1d6: (6) = 6
Narrator 3/28/2022 11:02 PM
This is a Field Operations campaign.
11:05 PM
The siege has cracked the city wide open. The last Huguenot holdouts and English stragglers flee La Rochelle in droves. Among their number, dozens of English commanders and French nobles, chiefly to blame for funding the multi-year rebellion, try to slip out amidst the filthy peasantry. The army, such as it is in the Fall, has been assigned inspection duty in the field, searching the endless lines of refugees for persons of interest.
Narrator 3/28/2022 11:12 PM
Deploying: Bg. Gen. @Curtis Sinnoch leading the 1st Brigade, including in the Picardy Musketeers Captains @CitrusNotes and @Blastagarded, and Pvt. @Ghôst Touchard. Lt. Col. @Jules Lavelle commanding the Royal Foot Guards. @Joseph Bousain has volunteered as a private in the Frontier Regiment. (edited)
11:15 PM
The Frontier Regiment is commanded by Col. Thomas Alard, a savvy veteran of the Spanish Campaigns.
11:16 PM
@Curtis Sinnoch, as senior officer on the field, you may set the tone of the endeavor.
Curtis Sinnoch 3/28/2022 11:21 PM
Curtis paced the halls of Bothwells - his need to stay in Paris during the Summer campaign now unleashing months of pent up rage and frustration upon the poor, unsuspecting English. An orderly meekly started to suggest holding some strength back and was silenced with a chopping motion Composing himself, Curtis heads to the 1st battalion HQ and sends his men to gather any and all available soldiers and officers, dragging some unwilling from their beds as needed "We are the finest of France! We will not allow the English to sully our lands any further! At dawn, the entire 1st brigade will mobilize and quash this uprising for good! I believe the term is "overwhelming firepower" yes? What others may otherwise seem as a troublesome bother, I see as an insidious attempt to poison France herself from within! No quarter will be given, and none shall be expected! Brave sons of France - you've been called, to deliver upon our enemy a DIVINE RETRIBUTION! With each musket shot, LET THUNDER BELLOW! With each sabre thrust, LET LIGHTNING STRIKE! We SHALL NOT, WILL NOT allow these filth to continue to contaminate our grounds! We ride! WE RIDE TO GLORY!"
1
Narrator 3/28/2022 11:22 PM
One week later, the 1st Brigade stands in bored ranks, blithely processing one refugee after another. @Curtis Sinnoch, give any relevant orders to your colonels and roll for your result.
Curtis Sinnoch 3/28/2022 11:27 PM
"Men, the enemy is insidious. They will play the part of the lowly peasant until you turn your backs, then your throat is CUT! Keep your men diligent and observant! Frequent guard changes to keep our processors sharp, with lookouts both forward and aft of the stations to alert of potential issues. I want a second task force of troops behind the primary processing stations at the ready should they need to support, or better yet flank an opposing force. We know the Narrator is a Jackass, and you have full permission to hunt down his kin. You've all trained and studied well, and all have full permission to deviate from orders to hunt targets of opportunity - the higher the rank, the more leniency will be granted. For FRANCE!" (edited)
Curtis Sinnoch
used /roll

Narrator 3/28/2022 11:32 PM
Your colonels listen closely, taking your words at face value, and the screams of peasants who've lost their homes and livelihoods can be heard from your checkpoints for days. In spite of it all, other than a few coins of English currency and a single English soldier in a dress doing his best French accent, your troops fail to do much more than terrorize their own countrymen. Result is a 4 - Inconclusive.

Maj. Jean Ouvrard, commanding the Picardy Musketeers, decides to personally intervene when he notices a greengrocer's cartful of cabbages seems oddly lumpy. He orders his men to perform a closer inspection.

Narrator 3/28/2022 11:58 PM
A fat French merchant is brought before Curtis, accused by his maid of having been a Huguenot collaborator. He listens carefully to the old woman's story as the man sweats in the dark of the field tent. Having heard enough, Curtis dismisses her and prepare to deliver the man his judgment. "Please, sir," he begs, "I have great riches hidden in the hills. Wealth beyond your dreams! I will pay you, just let me go!" The man falls to his knees, and Curtis nods slightly. "Give me your treasures, and I will show you mercy." The merchant, walking ahead, leads Curtis and his retinue out into the night. After ten minutes' walk, they come upon a small spring surrounding by a little copse of trees. The merchant runs his hands over one of the trees, then gasps in delight, yanking the side of the tree open. Immediately, hundreds of gold coins spill out from the hollowed trunk, glittering in the torchlight. The merchant smiles broadly. "There you are, sir. I give all this to you. Only let me live." Curtis frowns from atop his horse. "There is a problem here, scoundrel. This is traitor's money; it belongs to the crown. It is not yours to give." The merchant still has a confused expression on his face as Curtis gives the order, and his adjutant slits the sympathizer's throat. As a reward for the vast riches he's recovered, Curtis receives a permanent promotion, and a 50 crown bonus.

Blastagarded 3/29/2022 12:01 AM
Capt. Blastagarde takes his General's orders to heart. After all, for the past three months he has had nothing else to do but privately secure prototypes of various armaments from his family's business for his Company... along with an ample supply of regular munitions, including a personal cannon for any soldier who requested one. And so, as the refugees lined up for inspection, those who found themselves in the line for Company F were questioned not at sword point or with the barrel of a musket pointed at their head, but in front of a wall of cannons backed by musketeers which ensured any English invader who tried to flee would find themselves quickly and overwhelmingly surrounded by an insurmountable quantity of gun power propelled munitions ranging from pistol pellets to cannon balls. A couple of his soldiers even opted for an experimental miniature ballista which fired from a series of five auto-loaded javelins in under three seconds. And so, but for a few volleys triggered by either English scum; the overly excited; or, simply the purely terrified, the line for Company F proceeded in a largely orderly manner...

Narrator 3/29/2022 10:04 AM
Having hidden behind a rock and successfully avoided the wild ambush, Côme wanders over to observe the cleanup efforts and see if he can pick up any leftovers from the tontine. Meanwhile, the greengrocer stares in horror at what's left of his bloody, dismembered produce. Côme, noticing the man's distress, approaches to offer a consoling word. The greengrocer turns on him, livid, and hisses "MY CABBAGESSSSSSS!" before baring his teeth and going straight for Côme's throat. Côme fights him off, but not without losing a few bits of skin in the process. He applies a dirty bandage and thinks nothing more of the matter. The wounds swiftly turn gangrenous, and he expires in a hospital tent a few days later. (edited)

10:25 AM
The campaign wraps up, and each officer receives a personal note of thanks from the Cardinal for his part in the siege and ending the Protestant menace. "Never again will the shores of France be sullied by the presence of those who do not worship Christ in accordance with the doctrine of the Pope in Rome," reads the rose-scented missive. The European theater of this bloody, all-consuming war is at an end.
Blastagarded 5/13/2022 3:18 PM
Having received his orders, Albert kisses his new beau farewell as his carriage is loaded with new prototypes. The final large box which required four men to lift it bears the simple marking, "Le Vaporisateur"
Narrator 6/5/2022 4:04 PM
The Winter Campaign
4:04 PM
The Minister of War is especially pleased to learn that @everyone has volunteered!
Narrator 6/5/2022 4:13 PM
Bg. Gen. @Curtis Sinnoch commands the 1st Brigade of Foot, including Cpts. @CitrusNotes and @Blastagarded with the Picardy Musketeers, led by Maj. Ghislain Maitron. Lt. Col. @Jules Lavelle leads the Royal Foot Guards. The Frontier Regiment, led by Col. Lucien Lièvremont, a seasoned and celebrated officer, has awarded temporary commissions to Lt. Col. @Jean Renault and his Crown Prince Cuirassiers 1st Squadron, as well as Cpt. @Abelard Soucy. Sub. @Slade Imbee and Pvt. @Joseph Bousain fill the ranks.
Narrator 6/5/2022 4:20 PM
Henri, Duke of Rohan, a fiendish Huguenot leader, has learned nothing from the fall of La Rochelle and the terrible suffering the Protestants brought down upon themselves there. Seeing the folly of resisting Royal forces at sea, he's refocused his efforts inland, marching several armies overland at great speed to reach Huguenot strongholds in the South of France. One royal highway, running alongside the river Creuse, has been identified as the most likely avenue of escape for Rohan's remaining forces. The Frontier Regiment and volunteers have been deployed to intercept, and hopefully destroy, the Huguenots before they can return to their bases of support in the South.
4:22 PM
Narrator
used /roll
Dice Golem BOT 6/5/2022 4:22 PM
1d6: (3) = 3
Narrator 6/5/2022 4:23 PM
This is an Assault campaign.
4:25 PM
Reconnoitering cavalry report the Huguenots en route as expected. Bg. Gen. Sinnoch and Col. Maitron meet the evening before the armies will make contact to draw up the plan of battle. Over multiple servings of wine, Col. Maitron suggests a pincer movement, with his diverse forces on the right, and the 1st Brigade on the left. He imagines it will drive the enemy into the river.
4:25 PM
@Curtis Sinnoch, please give your orders and roll.
Blastagarded 6/7/2022 5:28 PM
Curtis Sinnoch 6/8/2022 7:48 AM
"Col. Maitron, I agree our best bet is to force the craven Huguenots towards the river is one of our best bets. However, I wish to make sure we utilize our best assets to ensure we inflict as much pain and suffering upon our foes as possible. To that end, I wish to use our Cavalry along the road to harass our foes long before the actual battle commences, hitting and running, drawing our their lines while the Frontier Regiment sabotages supply lines. " "We will launch our final attack here, where the river is shallow to the side closest to highway, but plunges unexpectedly towards the far bank. We will line up the Royal Foot Guard just inside the treeline, poised to rip the enemy apart as they flounder in deeper waters. What say you?"
Curtis Sinnoch 6/8/2022 9:33 AM
@Jules Lavelle you have final discretion on how to deploy your troops along the water line, but I would recommend keeping a small contingent of sharp shooters available to engage in specialty targets as they introduce themselves. Have them focus on the opposing bank to fire on incoming threats to allow the rank and file time to engage the masses. Godspeed.
Curtis Sinnoch
used /roll
Dice Golem BOT 6/8/2022 9:33 AM
1d6: (3) = 3
Narrator 6/8/2022 10:02 AM
Bg. Gen. Sinnoch's plan takes the disorganized enemy almost completely unawares. Already exhausted from a long march after a terrible defeat, and with nighttime raids and from Royal cavalry, and the resulting hunger, keeping everyone on edge, morale among the Protestant troops is at rock-bottom even before the bulk of the Royal forces present themselves. Only a few battalions, personally commanded by Rohan himself, manage to organize themselves quickly enough to provide any sort of resistance. The river runs red with the blood of their comrades as the battle wears on. The result is 2 - Bloody victory. (edited)
10:05 AM
Col. Maitron, seeking sensible glory for his Musketeers, holds them back from the battle until victory is assured. As the enemy begins to break, he finally orders the charge.
Narrator
used /roll
Dice Golem BOT 6/8/2022 10:05 AM
1d6: (1) = 1
Narrator 6/8/2022 10:08 AM
His delayed charge is timed almost perfectly with the Royal Foot Guard's major volley. Perfectly arrayed in defilade as per standard tactics, the RFG have aimed high to decapitate any mounted officers. There is a special prize out for any round that takes Rohan himself in the head. The RFG's best marksman, spotting the dour Duke towards the rear, takes careful aim, lights his musket, and pulls the trigger. At just that moment, the Picardy Musketeers leap onto the hill, taking dozens of balls that had been intended for the enemy in their own backs instead. The Musketeer assault collapses, and the RFG rush to help their fallen allies. The result is 5 - Repulsed.
Jules Lavelle 6/8/2022 10:09 AM
A shambles! Bg. Gen. Sinnoch's perfect plan -- ruined! Forget this nonsense, men! Leave those Musketeer fools to their fates and follow me, for the GLORY of FRANCE! CHAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAARGE!
Jules Lavelle
used /roll
Dice Golem BOT 6/8/2022 10:09 AM
1d6: (1) = 1
1
Narrator 6/8/2022 10:13 AM
Jules Lavelle charges forward on his horse, not taking the time to look back. His men, who had settled in for a comfortable, long-range engagement, are taking their time preparing for a charge. This is time Lavelle himself has not taken. Realizing his mistake, he turns his horse to rush back to his own lines, bellowing at his men to get to their foot. Lavelle's horse trips over the corpse of a slain Musketeer, and they both go down in a surprised tumble. Worried for their commander, the mass of Guardsmen toss aside their arms and rush out to help -- just in time for the Duke of Rohan's small cavalry contingent to rush in from the side, sowing chaos and bloody slaughter among the confused Guardsmen. Lavelle can do nothing but watch as the flower of French chivalry, entrusted to his care, is cut down before his very eyes. The result is 5 - Repulsed.
10:14 AM
Col. Lièvremont plays it by-the-book as usual, securing ground and enforcing his flanks before advancing. The Frontier Regiment encircles the foe and steadily pushes him back into the river, where the Protestants, feeling their boots getting wet, panic.
Narrator
used /roll
Dice Golem BOT 6/8/2022 10:14 AM

Narrator 6/8/2022 10:17 AM
But there is only one thing more dangerous than a loose wolf, and that is a cornered wolf. With certain death at their backs, the enemy fight like demons, and the Frontier Regiment lacks the cohesion to present any kind of solid resistance to an actual counter-assault. The volunteers and penal battalions begin dropping like flies, and the military reality of plans and contact with the enemy rears its ugly head once more. The plan hasn't survived, and neither will most of the Royal forces, as the Protestants cut a bloody swathe to freedom. The result is 5 - Repulsed.

Blastagarded 6/8/2022 10:40 AM
While Albert's men may have been cut down by miscoordinated friendly fire, at the very least they completed their assigned task and positioned "Le Vaporisateur" with an unobstructed view of the enemy. The monstrosity of six full barreled cannons held together by an aiming mechanism was intimidating on its own. But its true horror lay in its black powdered filled cannon balls designed to explode on impact... in theory. Requiring four men to operate and one brave soul to be standing next to it when it fired, Albert was not about to let anyone else take the glory. Albert orders the targeting of the most likely location of the Duke and, when ready, takes position beside his opus to fire it personally.
10:40 AM
/roll 1d6
Dice Golem BOT 6/8/2022 10:40 AM
Albert Blastagarde rolled 1d6: (4) = 4
Narrator 6/8/2022 10:49 AM
The grotesque apparatus lets out a terrible scream of overstressed iron. Two of the balls explode in their barrels, but by some miracle of metallurgy, the tubes mostly contain the force, merely splitting apart at the ends. Three of the other four balls sail out into the mass of forces, missing Rohan and his small force, but impacting amidst the densest concentration of Protestant soldiery. The effect is immediate and horrifying. Weapons, limbs, and surprised faces, some still attached to their heads, fly in every direction. Men who haven't bathed in weeks suddenly find themselves showered in a black liquid of mixed composition: one part river water, one part mud, and the rest the innards of their fellows. The final ball goes high, soaring across the river entirely, to where the Royal cavalry are patiently waiting to cut down any survivors fleeing across the river. The ball fails to explode, but instead bounces along the ground, where it finds ample choice of horse legs to snap like brittle twigs in its passing. The screams of maimed horses fill the air wherever the screams of men go quiet. The result is 3 - Pyrrhic victory.

Blastagarded 6/8/2022 10:51 AM

Jules Lavelle 6/8/2022 10:59 AM
The iron horror has done its work, and can now rest. But there is no rest for the makeshift scaffolding constructed to support it and assist its aim. Certain compromises had to be made to accommodate something so unwieldy that could also be readily turned, raised, and lowered. Recognizing the unsteadiness of the prototype's base, Blastagarde had ordered it reinforced with long planks assembled into trusses on either side. His engineering accounted for the stress of firing, and they handled the force without any trouble. Just one of these planks was torn away by a splitting barrel. Just one was enough. As Blastagarde surveys the carnage wrought by his creation, it teeters. His men's shouts of terror aren't enough to distract him from reveling in his success. His mind fills with visions of the transformation of warfare, of artillery becoming so powerful men can't move from trenches without being, as he puts it, vaporized. The harbinger of the future slowly, implacably, inevitably descends on its inventor, who is, far from vaporized, flattened by his inability to get out of the way of progress.
3
Curtis Sinnoch 6/8/2022 11:16 AM
Curtis surveils the carnage unfolding from a makeshift scaffolding hidden in the trees near his former RFG. He curses at the folly of Maitron, and curses his own misfortune that he will have to spend time writing letters to families of lost officers. He’ll order several bottles of fine Chablis, for gifts of condolence, and for himself. He continues to survey the area, sending messages via runners to better adjust firing positions and reposition troops as needed. He even fires a pot shot or two as the opportunity allows, aiming to sow calculated discord where possible. This is not his battle to obtain glory, but to support his soldiers and ensure the filthy Protestants are scoured from the land (edited)

Joseph Bousain 6/8/2022 5:45 PM
Bleary eyed from a wine soaked night with some of the other D company privates, Joseph finds himself marching near the back of the company, just in front of Cpt Matthieu Hachette, a man used to commanding from the back. Although Joseph hardly possesses the greatest military mind of his age, the snatches he catches of his Cpt's plans (Well, I'm sure the Huguenots won't think to hide in those trees, Artillery? Never could stand the stuff myself, far too loud, I'm sure we'll be fine with a good old fashioned infantry charge, Ah yes, but if the marsh blocks off our retreat, our men will fight all the harder!) do not fill him with confidence. From the corner of his eye, Joseph catches a glimpse of "Le Vaporisateur" and is immediately entranced. The private next to him has a rather different reaction. "MONSTROSITY!! What an infernal machine!" He yelps involuntarily. At this, Hachette springs up in his saddle. "Talking in the ranks?? What's this!? Why aren't you disciplining that man subaltern?" The platoon's subaltern, long tired of Hachette's inconsistent disciplinary approach sighs and reaches for the private. At this, Joseph spies an opportunity, even with his short time in the company, he is familiar with Hachette's bizarrely idiosyncratic approach to the discipline and the chain of command, and knows well his fondness for volunteers. Joseph volunteers to discipline the man himself. Hachette is pleased by this display of enthusiasm and is sure to make a note of the name Joseph gives him (Albert Demachy). Joseph grabs his victim and marches off into the forest to 'discipline' him, but instead leads him up to "Le Vaporisateur" in the hope of being able to witness it in action. As they watch the machine collapse, Joseph decides instead to steal some of the spare ammunition which he hopes to lob into the crowds of Huguenots.
1

CitrusNotes 6/8/2022 8:49 PM
Having been merely grazed in the back by stray bullets and only lightly trampled by confused horsemen, the VERY brief loss of consciousness caused by the shockwave of the experimental seige weapon was the final sign Armand needed. Clearly luck was on his side. With this irrefutable message from on high, and not at all a concussion-induced fantasy, Armand quietly gathers the few remaining able-bodied members of his Battalion and steals through the underbrush to form a surprise attack at the rear of the foe. "No one will expect us coming if we just slip in between them and the river!" He thinks, through a painful mental fog. "Besides, I am dreadfully thirsty and could really use a rinse to investigate the blood stains on this uniform. Surely, they can't ALL be from me..."

Despite the dangers and the battle frenzy of the pinned Protestants, there is simply no effective means of fighting on two flanks. As Rohan's troops are whittled down, the Musketeers begin suffering more harm from friendly bayonets than Protestant blades. The fighting is fierce, but over mercilessly quickly, even though only a few of the Royal forces are left standing. The result is 3 - Pyrrhic victory.

As the battle winds down, Armand sees a rider approaching. Assuming him to be Royal reinforcements, Cpt. Dufour raises his arm in greeting. In response, the Duke of Rohan swings his saber, lopping off the offending limb as he rides off down the river. Armand collapses to the ground, clutching at the stump while his remaining men shout impotent obscenities at the fleeing Huguenot leader. His blood mingles with the waters of the Creuse, as his spirit mingles with the great French heroes who gave their lives in defense of their home.

Narrator 6/9/2022 9:21 AM
Slade Imbee finds himself face-to-face with an enemy officer of great distinguishment. The man wields a rapier in one hand, and the other bears a state-of-the-art hand cannon. The fuse is already lit when they engage, and the mud makes agile maneuver impossible. The officer raises the gun, and Slade senses his doom approaching. The weapon fires, and time seems to slow down. Slade's instincts scream to duck, but he knows he's too late. Instead, he forces his body to twist, and by divine providence, the ball whizzes harmlessly past him, impacting in the mud some ten yards behind. The officer fumbles to reload his weapon, a fatal error. Slade throws mud in his enemy's face and tackles him to the ground, where he begins choking him out. Just as the life goes out of the officer's eyes, an explosive shell from another experimental device impacts directly on Slade's back. He, and his victory, are never known again. (edited)

Wisely ignoring the orders from the Frontier Regiment, Jean leads his cavalry down the road, where they engage a separate portion of the enemy force, peeling away a major section of reinforcements that might have turned the tide. Their sacrifice to distract the bulk of Rohan's might is heroic, but still a sacrifice -- no amount of maneuver or skilled riding can overcome the sheer numbers on the side of the Protestants, who nevertheless break when Rohan himself rushes back to order a withdrawal. The result is 3 - Pyrrhic victory
